_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: Born to erase a monarchy, he outlived the revolution that made him.

museum_objects.title: First Peseta Coin (Hispania Design)
museum_objects.description: The first official peseta coin, designed by Luis Marchionni, featuring the allegorical figure of Hispania.
museum_objects.significance: Carlos was born out of revolution — this coin replaced royal portraits with an idea, a symbol of a country trying to imagine itself without a king.
museum_objects.museum: Heritage Auctions (referenced example) / Museo Casa de la Moneda
museum_objects.license: Museo Casa de la Moneda terms
museum_objects.plain_language: The first official peseta coin, made right after Spain's queen was overthrown, showing a symbolic woman instead of a royal portrait.
museum_objects.institution: Heritage Auctions (referenced example) / Museo Casa de la Moneda
museum_objects.licence: Museo Casa de la Moneda terms

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

display_alternatives.title: Cupón de la ONCE dedicado a la peseta.jpg

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: Goya's Saturn Devouring His Son
museum_objects.description: One of Francisco Goya's darkest 'Black Paintings,' depicting the Roman god Saturn devouring his own child.
museum_objects.significance: Painted decades before Carlos existed, but it captures exactly the mood of the era that created him — a country consuming its own past to make room for something new.
museum_objects.plain_language: A famous, disturbing painting by Goya showing a god eating his own son — capturing the dark, chaotic mood of Spain right before the peseta was created.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.title: Museo Casa de la Moneda Collection
museum_objects.description: Spain's national mint museum, housing coins and printing plates spanning centuries of Spanish currency.
museum_objects.significance: This museum is where Carlos's entire life story — from revolutionary coin to today — is kept safe in one building.
museum_objects.accession_number: Museo Casa de la Moneda general collection
museum_objects.license: Museo Casa de la Moneda terms
museum_objects.plain_language: Spain's national coin museum, where the whole history of the peseta is kept and displayed.
museum_objects.accession: Museo Casa de la Moneda general collection
museum_objects.licence: Museo Casa de la Moneda terms

museum_objects.title: FNMT Historical Peseta Printing Plates
museum_objects.description: Original printing plates used by the Fábrica Nacional de Moneda y Timbre to produce peseta banknotes.
museum_objects.significance: These plates are the literal machinery of Carlos's identity — the exact tools that stamped his image onto every note Spaniards ever carried.
museum_objects.museum: Fábrica Nacional de Moneda y Timbre (FNMT)
museum_objects.plain_language: The actual metal plates used to print peseta banknotes, kept by Spain's official currency printer.
museum_objects.institution: Fábrica Nacional de Moneda y Timbre (FNMT)

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.title: Puerta del Sol Historical Photographs
museum_objects.description: Historical photographs of Madrid's central square, site of numerous political upheavals tied to Spain's changing governments and currencies.
museum_objects.significance: Every major turning point in Carlos's life — revolutions, republics, dictatorships — played out a few steps from this square.
museum_objects.museum: Referenced via Spanish national photo archives
museum_objects.plain_language: Old photos of Madrid's most famous square, where many of Spain's biggest political changes played out.
museum_objects.institution: Referenced via Spanish national photo archives

world_map.map_description: Carlos was born in the chaos of Spain's 1868 revolution and lived through republics, dictatorship, and democracy before retiring for the euro in 2002.

nodes.label: Madrid (Museo Casa de la Moneda)
nodes.description: Spain's capital and home to the national mint museum documenting the peseta's full history.

nodes.label: Puerta del Sol
nodes.description: The central square at the heart of nearly every major political upheaval in modern Spanish history.

nodes.label: Cádiz
nodes.description: A key port city during the 1868 revolution that toppled Queen Isabella II and set the stage for the peseta's creation.

nodes.label: Barcelona
nodes.description: Spain's second major economic hub, deeply tied to the peseta's use in trade and industry.

nodes.label: Frankfurt (ECB)
nodes.description: Where the peseta's story ended in 2002 with the adoption of the euro.
